step_by_step_reasoning: The whiteboard shows a video conferencing and recording processing solution. First, 'Users' (agents and clients) connect to a WebRTC video platform hosted on 'EC2'. Raw recordings from EC2 are uploaded to an initial 'S3' bucket. This upload triggers notifications to 'SQS'. 'Fargate' tasks poll 'SQS' to process the raw videos using FFmpeg, encoding them to HLS and saving them to a secondary processed 'S3' bucket. Finally, the processed videos are transitioned or archived to 'S3' Glacier for long-term storage as indicated by the final arrow.

graph.name: Lancome Technology Video Conferencing and Recording Processing
graph.notes: WebRTC video conferencing architecture hosted on EC2 with serverless media transcoding on ECS Fargate triggered via SQS queue and long-term storage on S3 Glacier.

nodes.name: Agents & Clients
nodes.notes: Insurance agents and claiming clients connecting via mobile/web.

nodes.name: OpenVideo on EC2
nodes.notes: Hosts WebRTC video platform and records raw sessions to local disk.

nodes.name: Raw Recordings S3
nodes.notes: Stores uploaded raw recordings from the EC2 instance.

nodes.name: Event Queue
nodes.notes: Buffers transcoding tasks triggered by S3 upload events.

nodes.name: ECS Fargate Transcoder
nodes.notes: Scalable container tasks running FFmpeg to transcode raw videos to HLS.

nodes.name: Processed Videos S3
nodes.notes: Stores transcoded HLS files for playback and review.

nodes.name: S3 Glacier
nodes.notes: Archival storage for long-term retention of video recordings.

edges.notes: Initiate and participate in WebRTC video calls.
Upload raw recording files to S3.
S3 event notification triggers SQS message.
Fargate tasks poll queue for processing jobs.
Upload processed HLS files to storage.
Transition old recordings to Glacier for cost-effective archiving.
